Facts.text: Romulus, first king of Rome, celebrates the first Roman triumph after his victory over the Caeninenses, following the Rape of the Sabine Women
Lucius Cornelius Sulla, at the head of a Roman Republic army, enters Athens, removing the tyrant Aristion who was supported by troops of Mithridates VI of Pontus
Roman emperor Maximianus introduces tetrarchy (rule of four people) elevating Galerius and Constantius Chlorus to Caesar
Gelasius I elected Catholic Pope; in office until his death in 496
St Felix III ends his reign as Catholic Pope
John VII begins his reign as Catholic Pope
The Roman Council at Estinnes decrees that Christian slaves may not be owned by Jews, fearing that the slaves may convert to Judaism
Balderik becomes bishop of Utrecht
Hulagu Khan, grandson of Genghis, conquers Damascus
Maillotin uprising against taxes in France
Pope Martinus I calls for crusade against the Hussite protestants
The Unitas Fratrum is established in the village of Kunvald, on the Bohemian-Moravian borderland. It is to date the second oldest Protestant denomination.
Blood bath at Vassy: Francois de Guise's troops open fire on Huguenot congregation, first event in Wars of Religion
Portuguese soldier Estácio de Sá founds the city of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
Puritan English parliamentary leader Sir Peter Wentworth confined in London Tower
The Uppsala Synod is summoned to confirm the exact forms of the Lutheran Church of Sweden
Battle at Smolensk: Polish King Wladyslaw IV beats Russians
Georgeana (York) in Maine becomes the first incorporated American city
Pennsylvania becomes first US state to abolish slavery (for newborns only)
Philadelphia Society for the Promotion of Agriculture organized
1st US census authorized
US Presidential Succession Act passed
1st National Meeting in the Hague
Ohio becomes 17th state of the Union
Embargo Act of 1807 (USA) repealed & Non-Intercourse Act signed
Egyptian king Muhammad Ali Pasha oversees ceremonial murder of 500
French Civil Code of Criminal law accepted by Netherlands Mamelukes in Cairo's Citadel
Sunday observance in Netherlands regulated by law
Adolphe Thiers becomes prime minister of France
Michigan becomes the first English-speaking jurisdiction to abolish the death penalty (except for treason against the state)
Archibald William Montgomerie, 13th Earl of Eglinton appointed Lord Lieutenant of Ireland.
German psychologist Friedrich Eduard Beneke disappears; two years later his remains are found in a canal near Charlottenburg.
SS City of Glasgow leaves Liverpool harbour with approximately 480 passengers and crew; she is never seen again
Present seal of San Francisco adopted (its 2nd)
Louis Ducos du Hauron patents movie machine (never built)
Rebecca Lee in the US becomes 1st African American woman to receive a medical degree
Howard University, Washington, D.C., chartered
Most of Nebraska becomes 37th US state (expanded later)
US postage stamps featuring scenes are issued for the first time, depicting a post horse and rider, a locomotive, a shield, an eagle, and a ship, the Adriatic
J Milton Turner named US minister to Liberia
Yellowstone becomes the world's first national park
E. Remington and Sons in Ilion, New York begins production of the first practical typewriter
US Congress passes Civil Rights Act; invalidated by Supreme Ct, 1883
Guernsey Cattle Club forms (Farmington Conn)
First winter ascent of Aneto, highest mountain in the Pyrenees
Library of Hawaii founded
The Anglo-Chinese School in Singapore is founded by Bishop William Oldham.
Diplomatic Appropriation Act, authorizes the US rank of ambassador
Major League Baseball Rules Committee rule that pitcher's mound must not be more than 15 inches higher than the baselines or home plate
1st US university school of nursing established, University of Minnesota
Wall of snow collapse in Cascade Mountain Range buries three passenger trains, killing 96, near Steven's Pass, Washington; 23 people survive the worst avalanche in US history
Isabella Goodwin appointed first US woman detective, NYC
U.S. Army Captain Albert Berry performs first (attached-type) parachute jump from an airplane
1First state law requiring bonding of officers & state employees, ND
Dutch Minister of War Hendrikus Colijn named director of British Petroleum
The Republic of China joins the Universal Postal Union.
Germany begins attacking ships in the Atlantic
1st federal land bank chartered in USA
US government releases the plain text of the "Zimmermann Telegram" to the public; Germany proposed reward to Mexico if they joined war against the US
The March 1st, or Samil Movement, begins in Korea: it is a demonstration of resistance to Japanese occupation
Buriat ASSR, in RSFSR, constituted
Miklós Horthy is elected Regent of Hungary, which had adopted a constitution on 28 February
Rwanda ceded to Great Britain
Sailors revolt in Kronstadt, Russia
Spin bowler Arthur Mailey takes 5 for 119 (record 36 wickets for series) to lead Australia to a 9 wicket 5th Test win over England in Sydney; first ever, 5-0 Ashes cricket series win for Australia
Allies occupy Ruhrgebied: killing railroad striker
Germany's prohibition of Communist Party KPD lifted

12 pro-independence revolutionaries captured in and around Chittagong in Bengal province, British India are sentenced to deportation for life, two to three-year prison terms with the remaining 32 being acquitted.
Bank holidays declared in 6 states, to prevent run on banks
Henry Pu Yi crowned Emperor Kang Teh of Manchuria
A strike occurs aboard the S.S. California, leading to the demise of the International Seamen's Union and the creation of the National Maritime Union.
Boulder (Hoover) Dam fully completed
1st permanent automobile license plates issued (Ct)
Governor Wouters innaugrates the radio station on the Dutch Antilles
US Steel raises workers' wages to $5 a day
Federal Trade Commission orders 8 manufacturers and the PGA of America to end their practice of golf ball price-fixing
Japanese Imperial Army ammunition dump exploded at Hirakata, Osaka, Japan, killing 94
Richard Wright's novel "Native Son" is published
1st US commercial FM radio station goes on the air, Nashville, Tennessee
Former University of Notre Dame star fullback Elmer Layden is named first Commissioner of the NFL
German troops invade Bulgaria
Rangers' goalie Dave Kerr becomes the 5th goaltender in NHL history to record 200 career victories when New York wins, 3-1 over the Canadiens at Montreal
3 day Battle of Java Sea ends, US suffers a major naval defeat
J Milton Cage Jr's "Imaginary Landscape No 3" premieres in Chicago
Japanese troops occupy Kalidjati airport in Java
Major League Baseball owners decide not to allow players in the military to play for their clubs when on furlough
Jewish old age home for disabled in Amsterdam raided
Massive strikes in northern Italian towns
U-358 sinks in Atlantic
British 43rd Division under General Hubert Essame occupies Xanten
Chinese 30th division occupies Hsenwi
US infantry regiment captures Mönchengladbach
British government nationalises and takes control of the Bank of England, after 252 years
Panama accepts its new constitution
International Monetary Fund begins operation
Indonesia seizes Yogyakarta from the Dutch
USSR issues golden roubles
Egyptian government-Ali Maher Pasha resigns
Helgoland, in North Sea, returned to West Germany by Britain
KAUZ TV channel 6 in Wichita Falls, TX (CBS) begins broadcasting
KTNT (now KSTW) TV channel 11 in Tacoma-Seattle, WA (IND) begins
WFBG TV (later WTAJ) channel 10 in Altoona, PA (CBS) begins broadcasting
WJZ-AM in NYC becomes WABC; WJZ-TV in Baltimore final transmission
4 Puerto Ricans open fire in US House of Representatives injuring 5 members
Rebellion in Egypt during visit of president Naguib to Khartoum, Sudan, 30 die
Israeli assault on Gaza, kills 48
KFAR (now KATN) TV channel 2 in Fairbanks, AK (ABC/NBC) 1st broadcast
Pakistan and India play out a draw in the 5th cricket Test at the National Stadium in Karachi; 5 Test series drawn 0-0
"Crazy Arms" single released by Ray Price - his 1st No.1 (Billboard Song of the Year 1956)
East Germany establishes the National People's Army (NVA) to serve as its armed forces
The International Air Transport Association finalizes a draft of the Radiotelephony spelling alphabet for the International Civil Aviation Organization.
Kokomo the Chimp becomes Today Show animal editor
KTWO TV channel 2 in Casper, WY (NBC/ABC/CBS) begins broadcasting
Samuel Alphonsus Stritch, is appointed Pro-Prefect of the Propagation of Faith and thus becomes the first American member of the Roman Curia
Archbishop Makarios returns to Cyprus after 3 years
Cellist Jacqueline du Prés debut in Wigmore Hall
American Airlines 707 plunges nose first into Jamaica Bay, NY, killing 95
Kresge Corporation openes the first Kmart store in Garden City, Michigan
US and British nuclear test experiment in Nevada
200,000 French mine workers strike
Gas explosion kills 28 in apartment complex in La Salle, Quebec
WPSX TV channel 3 in Clearfield, PA (PBS) begins broadcasting
Ba'ath-party takes power in Syria
Venera 3 becomes 1st man-made object to impact on a planet (Venus)
Dominica & St Lucia gain independence from Britain
MLB Commissioner William Eckert approves the Baseball Writers Association of America's plan to name a Cy Young Award recipient from both the National and American Leagues
Queen Elizabeth Hall in London's South Bank Centre opens
US House of Representatives expels Rep Adam Clayton Powell Jr (307 to 116)
WMET (now WHSW) TV channel 24 in Baltimore, MD (IND) 1st broadcast
High winds blow-off the Philadelphia Spectrum's roof, forcing the Flyers to play their final month of the NHL season on the road, with home games moved to Toronto, NYC and Quebec City
NBC's unprecedented on-air announcement, Star Trek will return
Pirate Radio Atlantis South (England) begins test transmitting
Political Party Radikalen (PPR) established in Netherlands
Vatican City's Apostolic Constitution of 1967 goes into effect
After 88 weeks, the Beatles' album "Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band" drops off the charts
Pirate Radio 259 begins operation off the French coast
Tuesdee Testa becomes the first female jockey to win a race at a major American Thoroughbred track when she takes out the 3rd race at Santa Anita Park aboard Buz On
Dutch speed skater Atje Keulen-Deelstra becomes Women's Allround World Champion at West Allis, Wisconsin, US, winning her first of four titles
End of US commercial whale hunting
Kreisky's Social Democrats win Austrian parliamentary election
Minnesota coach Charlie Burns becomes the final player/coach in NHL history, when he plays in the North Stars' 8-0 win over Toronto Maple Leafs; remains in role for final month of the season
Bomb attack on the Capitol in Washington, D.C.
Clandestine Radio Deutsche Reich (Germany) begins transmitting on FM
Club of Rome publishes report "Boundaries on the Growth"
David Rabe's "Sticks & Bones" premieres in NYC
KHMA TV channel 11 in Houma, LA (IND) begins broadcasting
Two Catholic teenagers shot dead by the Royal Ulster Constabulary while 'joy riding' in a stolen car in Belfast
Robyn Smith rides North Sea to victory in the Paumanok Handicap at Aqueduct Race Track in NYC to become the first American female jockey to win a stakes race
Watergate grand jury indicts 7 presidential aides
17th Grammy Awards: I Honestly Love You, Marvin Hamlisch win
Colour television transmission begins in Australia
Eagles' "Best of My Love" reaches #1
Bank of America adopts the name VISA for its credit cards
US extends territorial waters to 200 miles
Crispus and Constantine II, sons of Roman EmperorConstantine I, and Licinius the younger, son of Emperor Licinius, raised to rank of Caesar
Printing is completed ofErasmus' "Novum Instrumentum omne", first published New Testament in Greek
British admiral and explorerFrancis Drakesurprises and captures the Spanish treasure ship 'Nuestra Señora de la Concepción' off the coast of Peru, Drake's richest plunder
Pope Gregory XIV threatens to excommunicate French KingHenry IV
Writs are issued in February byCharles Iof England mandating that every county in England (not just seaport towns) pay ship tax by this date
Samuel de Champlainreclaims his role as commander of New France on behalf of Cardinal Richelieu
Richard SteeleandJoseph Addisonpublish the 1st edition of "The Spectator" in London to "enliven morality with wit, and to temper wit with morality"
LordJohn Russellintroduces the Reform Bill in the British House of Commons on the government's behalf to revamp the electoral system of England and Wales and increase the franchise
US PresidentJohn Tylersigns a resolution annexing the Republic of Texas
War of the Triple Alliance: Paraguayan canoes sink two Brazilian ironclads on the Rio Paraná
1st US edition of Sherlock Holmes' first story "A Study in Scarlet" byArthur Conan Doylepublished
Battle of Adwa: 80,000 Ethiopians destroy 20,000 Italians in Ethopia, killing two generals and capturing General Matteo Albertone
Henri Becquereldiscovers radioactivity
David Beattybecomes Rear-Admiral Commanding the Royal Navy's 1st Battlecruiser Squadron
Bank of Italy (laterBank of America) becomes an American National Bank
Paul Whiteman and His Orchestra record "Ol' Man River" for Victor Records featuringBing Crosby
The "Lindbergh kidnapping", 20 month oldson of CharlesandAnne Lindberghkidnapped from home in East Amwell, New Jersey, found dead 12 May
Italian boxerPrimo Carnerabeats American challenger Tommy Loughran by unanimous points decision at Madison Square Garden, NYC for the NYSAC and NBA heavyweight titles
Captain America, created by cartoonists Joe Simon andJack Kirby, is first published by Timely Comics (premiere issue released on December 20, 1940)
Commander of the SS,Heinrich Himmlerinspects Auschwitz concentration camp for the first time, orders it be expanded
Titoestablishes Second Proletarian Brigade to fight Axis occupation in Bosnia and Herzegovina

MLB Cleveland Indians ownerBill Veecksets up spring training camp in racially tolerant Phoenix, AZ in anticipation of signing team's first black players;Larry Dobyis first acquisition later in the season
Former world heavyweight boxing championJoe Louisretires with a 66-3-0 record including 52 KOs; defended the title a record 25 times
Chiang Kai-shekresumes the presidency of the Republic of China on Taiwan
Klaus Fuchssentenced in London to 14 years for atomic espionage
After an all-night movie and dinner session with his top advisers,Joseph Stalinsuffers a stroke and collapses. He dies four days later.
Future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielderTed Williamsfractures his collarbone on the first day of Boston Red Sox spring training; injury keeps him out of Boston lineup until May 15

21-year-old West Indian cricket all-rounderGarfield Sobersturns his maiden Test century into a world record 365no in the Windies' 3rd Test win over Pakistan in Kingston, Jamaica; Sobers and Conrad Hunte (260) 446 run partnership for 2nd wicket
US PresidentJFKestablishes the Peace Corps
Australian swimming authorities suspend triple Olympic gold medal winning sprinterDawn Fraserfor 10 years for misconduct at the 1964 Tokyo Olympics
Andrew Lloyd WebberandTim Rice's musical "Joseph and the Amazing Technicolor Dreamcoat" first performed as a 15-minute pop cantata at Colet Court School in London
Jim Morrisonallegedly exposes himself on stage at the Dinner Key Auditorium in Miami, Florida
New York Yankees legendMickey Mantleannounces his retirement due to persistent knee injuries and finishes an 18-season career with 536 home runs and a .298 batting average
Phil Espositohas a goal and assist in Boston Bruins' 8-5 win over NY Rangers to give him 99 points for the season, breaking the NHL record for most points in a season (97) held byStan Mikita
Boston'sBobby Orrbecomes the first defenseman in NHL history to score 25 goals in a season during a 3-1 Bruins win over the visiting St. Louis Blues
Charles Manson's album "Lie" is released

Harvest (UK) and Capitol Records (US) releasePink Floyd's 8th studio album "The Dark Side of the Moon"; it stays on the Billboard Top 200 Album chart for most of the next 14 years, selling over 50 million copies worldwide
Australian cricketing brothers Ian (145) andGreg Chappell(162no) record 264 partnership in 1st Test draw vs New Zealand at Wellington; Ian (121) and Greg (133) double up with centuries in 2nd innings
George Harrisonannounces his concert tour of US to begin in November
PhysicistStephen Hawkingpublishes his landmark paper "Black hole explosions" introducing idea of 'Hawking radiation' the idea black holes are not truly black because they omit heat
Charlie Chaplin's coffin and remains are stolen from a Swiss cemetery in an extortion plot
Sarah Goode, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba are interrogated after accusations that they were practitioners of witchcraft in Salem, Massachusetts Bay Colony
Continental Congress officially adopts the Articles of Confederation, the first constitution of the United States of America after ratification by the 13th state, Maryland

War of the Triple Alliancefinally ends with the Battle of Cerro Corá and the death of Paraguayan DictatorFrancisco Solano Lópezafter over 5 years of bloodshed between Paraguay and Argentina, Brazil and Uruguay
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltannounces success of the Yalta Conference
US explodes Castle Bravo, a 15-megaton hydrogen bomb at Bikini Atoll, which accidentally becomes the most powerful nuclear device ever detonated by the US
Martial, Roman poet and satirist known for his Epigrams, born in Bilbilis, Hispania  (d. 102) date disputed
Bai Juyi [Po Tjiu-i], Chinese Tang dynasty poet and governor of Hang-tsjow, born in Xinzheng, China (d. 846)
Isabel of Coimbra, Portuguese princess and Queen of Portugal (1447-55) as 1st wife of Alfonso V, born in Coimbra, Portugal (d. 1455)
Sandro Botticelli, Florentine Early Renaissance painter (Birth of Venus), born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. 1510) [birthdate disputed]
Vladislaus II, King of Bohemia (1471-1516) and King of Hungary (1490-1516), born in Kraków (d. 1516)
Il Bacchiacca [Franceso Ubertini], Italian Renaissance painter, born in Florence (d. 1557)
Albert V [the Generous], Duke of Bavaria (1550-79), born in Munich (d. 1579)
Rudolph Goclenius, German philosopher who popularized the term psychology, born in Korbach, Waldeck (d. 1628)
Jean-Charles de la Faille, Flemish mathematician and Jesuit priest, born in Antwerp (d. 1652)
Giovanni Francesco Milanta, Italian composer and organist, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (now Italy) (d. 1651)
Johann Balthasar Schupp, German poet and writer, born in Giessen (d. 1661)
John Pell, British mathematician and diplomat, born in Southwick, Sussex, England (d. 1685)
Ferdinand van Apshoven the Younger, Flemish painter, born in Antwerp (d. 1694) [date baptized]
Samuel Werenfels, Swiss theologian (reasonable orthodoxy), born in Basel, Swiss Confederacy (d. 1740)
Caroline of Brandenburg-Ansbach, wife of King George II of Great Britain, born in Ansbach, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1737)
Tsangyang Gyatso, 6th Dalai Lama (1697-1706), born at Urgelling Monastery (d. 1706)
Conrad Beissel, German born American religious leader and composer (founded Ephrata community), born in Eberbach (d. 1768)
Dieudonne Raick, Flemish composer and organist, born in Liège (d. 1764) baptism date
Josef Antonin Gurecky, Czech composer, born in Přerov (d. 1769)
Peregrinus Pogl, German composer, born in Sandau (d. 1788)
William Cushing, 2nd Chief Justice of the United States, born in Scituate, Massachusetts Bay, British America (d. 1810)
Pieter Valck, Dutch artist and sculptor, born in Leeuwarden (d. 1783)
François Buzot, French revolutionary leader, born in Évreux, France (d. 1794)
Jeremiah Ingalls, early North-American choir master and composer (Farewell Hymn), born in Andover, Massachusetts (d. 1838)
Armand-Emmanuel Trial, French pianist and comic opera composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1803)
Jacob Gottfried Weber, German composer, born in Freinsheim (d. 1839)
Gheorghe Asachi, Moldovian-Romanian author, poet, polyglot and humanist (1st newspaper in Moldavia), born in Hertsa, Principality of Moldavia (d. 1869)
Alexey Verstovsky, Russian composer, born in Kozlovsky Uyezd, Russia (d. 1862)
Wilford Woodruff, American Morman, fourth president of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints who ended polygamy, born in Farmington, Connecticut (d. 1898)
Robert Christie Buchanan, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1878)
Augustus Pugin, English architect known for his Gothic Revival style (interior of the Palace of Westminster), born in London (d. 1852)
Giovanni Duprè, Italian sculptor (Pieta), born in Siena (d. 1882)
George Davis, American Confederate politician, Attorney General of the Confederate States (1864-65), born near Wilmington, North Carolina (d. 1896)
Richard Redhead, English organist, and composer of sacred music, born in Harrow, England (d. 1901)
Joseph Hubert Reinkens, German Catholic bishop, leader of the Old Catholic dissidents, born in Burtscheid, Prussia (d. 1896)
Albin Francisco Schoepf, Polish-American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Podgórze, Kraków, Poland (d. 1886)
Charles Champion Gilbert, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Zanesville, Ohio (d. 1903)
John Thomas, Welsh composer and harpist, born in Bridgend (d. 1913)
James Fleming Fagan, American farmer and Condeferate Army Major General (Shiloh; Mark's Mill), born in Clark County, Kentucky (d. 1893)
Vittorio Bersezio [Carlo Nugelli], Italian playwright, born in Peveragno (d. 1900)
Hiram B. Granbury, American lawyer, county judge, and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Copiah County, Mississippi (d. 1864)
Friedrich Grutzmacher, German cellist and composer, born in Dessau, Duchy of Anhalt, German Confederation (d. 1903)
Matthias Joseph Scheeben, German theologist (Natur und Gnade), born in Meckenheim (d. 1888)
Andrew Strath, Scottish golfer (British Open 1865), born in St. Andrews, Fife (d. 1868)
Ion Creanga, Moldovian-born Romanian fairy tale author (Amintiti the Copilarie), born in Humulești, Principality of Moldavia (d. 1889)
William Dean Howells, American novelist, critic and editor (Atlantic), born in Martinsville, Ohio (d. 1920)
Gabriele dell' Addolorata [Francesco Possenti], Italian priest, saint and mystic, born in Assisi (d. 1862)
Laura Netzel (née Pistolekors), Finnish-Swedish pianist, composer, concert organizer, and humanitarian, born in Rantasalmi, Finland (d. 1927)
Blanche Bruce, American politician who was born a slave, (Sen-Miss, 1875-1881), born in Farmville, Virginia (d. 1898)
Romualdo Marenco, Italian composer, chiefly of ballet music (Excelsior), and conductor, born in Novi Ligure, Italy (d. 1907)
Nicholaos Gysis, Greek painter known for "Eros and the Painter", born in Sklavochori, island of Tinos (d. 1901)
Augustus Saint-Gaudens, American sculptor and designer (Saint Gaudens Double Eagle $20 gold piece), born in Dublin, United Kingdom (d. 1907)
Théophile Delcassé, French statesman (Entente Cordiale), born in Pamiers, France (d. 1923)
Paul Hines, American MLB outfielder (Triple Crown 1878, Providence Grays), born in Virginia (d. 1935)
Florimond Fonteyne, Belgian priest and politician (Volkseeuw), born in Houtem (d. 1923)
Georg Simmel, German philosopher and sociologist, born in Berlin, Prussia (d. 1918)
Alexander Golovin, Russian painter and stage designer, born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1930)
Alaska P. Davidson, America's first female FBI agent, born in Warren, Ohio (d. 1934)
Marie-Louise de Baufremont, wife of Prince Jean de Merode of Netherlands, born in Paris, France (d. 1955)
Henri de Baillet-Latour, Belgian aristocrat (3rd President International Olympic Committee 1925-42), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1942)
Gabriel Édouard Xavier Dupont, French composer, born in Caen, France (d. 1914)
Julian Myrick, American tennis promoter (West Side Tennis Club & Forest Hills Stadium), born in Murfreesboro, North Carolina (d. 1969)
Lytton Strachey, English biographer and critic (Benson Medal 1923), born in London (d. 1932)
Gwen Lally, English pageant master and actress (only played male roles), born in London, England (d. 1963)[1]
Ida Moore, American character actress (Mr. Music; Ma and Pa Kettle; Desk Set), born in Altoona, Kansas (d. 1964)
Tom Shevlin, American College Football Hall of Fame end (Consensus All-American 1902, 04, 05; National C'ship 1902, 05; Yale University), born in Muskegon, Michigan (d. 1915)
Lionel Atwill, British actor (The Mystery of the Wax Museum), born in Croydon, London, England (d. 1946)
Oskar Kokoschka, Austrian-British painter (Erasmus Prize 1960), born in Pöchlarn, Austria-Hungary (d. 1980)
Ewart Astill, England cricket all-rounder (9 Tests, Leicestershire), born in Ratby, Leicestershire, England (d. 1948)
Watsuji Tetsuro, Japanese historian and moral philosopher, born in Himeji, Japan (d. 1960)
Jan Duiker, Dutch architect (Zonnestral), born in The Hague, the Netherlands (d. 1935)
Billy Coutu, Canadian ice hockey defenceman (Stanley Cup 1924; Montreal Canadiens; only player banned from NHL for life, attacked referee 1927), born in North Bay, Ontario (d. 1977)
Mercedes de Acosta, American poet, playwright, costume designer, and socialite, born in New York (d. 1968)
Ryunosuke Akutagawa 'father of the Japanese short story', Japanese writer (Rashomon in Kappa), born in Kyōbashi, Japan (d. 1927)
Duke Keats, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame centre (WCHL First-Team All-Star 1921-26; Edmonton Eskimos; NHL: Chicago Black Hawks), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1972)
Dimitri Mitropoulos, Greek-American pianist, conductor (Minneapolis Symphony, 1937-49; New York Philharmonic, 1949-58), and composer, born in Athens, Greece (d. 1960)
Moriz Seeler, German writer , poet, and film producer, born in Greifenberg, Germany (d. 1942)
Erich von dem Bach-Zelewski, German military officer and high ranking SS commander in charge of Nazi security warfare, born in Lauenburg, German Empire (d. 1972)
Giorgos Seferis, Greek poet and diplomat (Strofi, Nobel Prize for Literature 1963), born in Vourla, Asia Minor, Ottoman Empire (d. 1971)
Keith Falkner, British bass-baritone singer (Director of the Royal College of Music), born in Sawston, England (d. 1994)
Paul Hartman, American actor (Petticoat Junction, Inherit the Wind), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1973)
Doris Hare, Welsh actress, born in Bargoed, Glamorgan, Wales (d. 2000)
Pol le Roy, Flemish writer (Stroom) and WWII collaborator, born in Hingene, Begium (d. 1983)
Albert Ammons, American jazz, blues, and boogie-woogie pianist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1949)
Terence De Marney, British actor (Lorna Doone; Doctor Who; The Immortal Gentleman), born in London, England (d. 1971)
Lois Moran, American actress (Waterfront), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1990)
Richard de Guide, Belgian composer, born in Basècles, Belgium (d. 1962)
Harry Golombek, British chess grandmaster and author (3-time British champion), born in London, England (d. 1995)
Gerald Emmett Carter, Canadian Catholic clergyman (Archbishop of Toronto, 1978-90; Cardinal 1979-2003), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2003)
Helmut Gernsheim, German photographer, collector and historian, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1995)
Archie Gumede, South African anti-apartheid activist and politician (ANC), born in Pietermaritzburg, South Africa (d. 1998)
Harry Caray, American Baseball Hall of Fame broadcaster (Chicago Cubs), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1998)
Ralph Ellison, American writer (Invisible Man; Shadow and Cast: Juneteenth), born in Oklahoma City (d. 1994)
Joop Klant, Dutch economist and author, born in Warmenhuizen, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Bing Devine, American baseball executive (GM St. Louis Cardinals 1957-64 [World Series 1964], NY Mets), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2007)
Robert Lowell, American poet and pacifist (Lord Weary's Castle, Near the Ocean), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1977)
Duncan White, Ceylonese athlete (first Olympic medallist from Ceylon, silver 400m hurdles 1948), born in Lathpandura, Sri Lanka (d. 1998)
Gladys Spellman, American educator, politician, and representative for Maryland's 5th congressional district, born in New York (d. 1988)
Hank Wyse, American baseball pitcher (MLB All Star 1945; Chicago Cubs), born in Lunsford, Arkansas (d. 2000)
Roger Delgado, English actor (Doctor Who, Agent 8 3/4, Hot Enough for June), born in London, England (d. 1973)
João Goulart, Brazilian politician (President of Brazil. 1961-64), born in São Borja, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil (d. 1976)
Howard Nemerov, American poet, two-time US Poet Laureate and novelist (Blue Swallows), born in New York City (d. 1991)
Max Bentley, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame center (Hart Trophy 1946; Chicago Blackhawks), born in Delisle, Saskatchewan (d. 1984)
Cameron Argetsinger, American auto racing executive (created Watkins Glen Grand Prix Race Course; F1 US Grand Prix 1961-80), born in Youngstown, Ohio (d. 2008)
Jack Clayton, English film director (The Great Gatsby), born in Brighton, England (d. 1995)
Richard Wilbur, 2nd US Poet Laureate (Ceremony, Walking to Sleep), born in New York City (d. 2017)
Terrence Cooke, American Roman Catholic Cardinal, Archbishop of New York, born in New York City (d. 1983)
Fred Scolari, American basketball point guard (NBA All-Star 1952, 53; Washington Capitols; Baltimore Bullets), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2002)
William Gaines, American publisher (MAD Magazine), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1992)
Péter Kuczka, Hungarian writer, poet, and editor of science fiction, born in Székesfehérvár, Hungary (d. 1999)
(Donald) "Deke" Slayton, American US Air Force pilot and NASA astronaut (Mercury Seven; Apollo–Soyuz Test Project), born in Sparta, Wisconsin (d. 1993)[1]
Lucine Amara [Armaganian], American concert and opera soprano (Metropolitan Opera, 1950-91), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2024)[1]
Allan Stanley, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame defenceman (Stanley Cup 1962-64, 67; NHL All-Star 1960, 61, 66; Toronto Maple Leafs), born in Timmins, Ontario (d. 2013)
